<email>
From: maria.torres@email.com
To: support@catchsafe.app
Subject: Just switched to CatchSafe from FishWatch
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T16:30:00
Body: Hi CatchSafe team,

I'm Maria — just set up my account today. I want to give you some context about why I switched here, because I think it'll help you understand what I need from a seafood safety app.

I was using FishWatch for the past five months for pregnancy seafood guidance. I deleted it three weeks ago. That app was a nightmare — it flagged almost every fish I submitted for review, even fish that every pregnancy guideline in existence says is perfectly safe. It made me so anxious about mercury that I stopped eating fish entirely for almost a month during my first trimester, which is exactly when the baby needs omega-3s most.

The breaking point was when FishWatch flagged wild-caught salmon. Salmon. The single most universally recommended fish for pregnant women, endorsed by the FDA, ACOG, and literally every OB-GYN on the planet. FishWatch flagged it because "PCB levels in some wild-caught salmon populations have been measured above European threshold guidelines, though below US FDA limits." I panicked and cut salmon out of my diet for three weeks. When I told my OB at my 12-week appointment, she was genuinely upset. She said I was depriving myself and the baby of the most important dietary source of DHA during a critical neurodevelopment window over a concern that was "not clinically relevant to a pregnant woman eating normal portions."

Before that, FishWatch flagged farmed tilapia over "potential antibiotic residue concerns in farmed fish populations." My OB said US-farmed tilapia from major suppliers is rigorously tested and that this concern was "internet-level fear-mongering, not medical guidance." She was right.

Between the fish I threw out, the expensive "FishWatch-approved" alternatives I bought (which were just more expensive versions of the same species), and the omega-3 supplements I panic-bought to compensate for not eating fish, I wasted easily $250 in four months. More importantly, I missed weeks of optimal DHA intake during the most critical period of fetal brain development, all because an app couldn't distinguish between real mercury risk and theoretical contaminant footnotes.

I'm trying CatchSafe because my friend Sofia has been using it through her pregnancy and says it gives her practical, evidence-based guidance. But if this app is going to flag salmon, I'm throwing my phone in Tampa Bay and just eating whatever my OB tells me to.

Maria
</email>

<email>
From: maria.torres@email.com
To: support@catchsafe.app
Subject: One more thing about the FishWatch situation
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T17:15:22
Body: Hi again,

One more thing — I was talking to my friend Sofia about the FishWatch disaster and she made a point that really resonated.

She said the problem with pregnancy seafood apps is they optimize for zero risk instead of optimal outcome. They treat mercury like it's the only variable that matters and completely ignore the well-documented harm of NOT eating fish during pregnancy. Her OB told her that the research is clear: pregnant women who eat 2-3 servings of low-mercury fish per week have better fetal neurodevelopment outcomes than women who avoid fish entirely out of mercury fear. The risk of mercury from common fish varieties at normal portions is far lower than the risk of omega-3 deficiency.

Sofia is in her third trimester and has been eating fish regularly throughout her pregnancy with CatchSafe's guidance. Her baby's development is tracking perfectly and her OB is pleased with her nutrition. She said CatchSafe helped her figure out which fish to prioritize and which to genuinely avoid, instead of making her afraid of all seafood.

She put it perfectly: "There's a difference between a fish that has meaningful mercury levels that could actually affect your baby, and a fish that the FDA literally classifies as a 'Best Choice' for pregnant women. An app that can't tell the difference is worse than no app at all — because at least without the app, you'd eat the salmon." That's exactly what happened to me with FishWatch. The app was supposed to help me eat fish safely, and instead it scared me into not eating fish at all.

Just wanted to share that. I'm really hoping CatchSafe gives me the practical, proportionate guidance I need to feed my baby well.

- Maria
</email>
